heroOverview: Fascioliasis is a zoonotic liver fluke infection caused by Fasciola hepatica or Fasciola gigantica — flatworms that migrate through the liver to the bile ducts, triggering tissue destruction and intense eosinophilia. The WHO estimates 2.4 million people are infected worldwide, with a further 180 million at risk, predominantly in Latin America, Europe, the Middle East, and parts of Africa and Asia. Humans acquire the parasite by eating freshwater vegetation — most often watercress — contaminated with infective metacercariae. The clinical course runs in two distinct phases: an acute migratory phase with fever, right-upper-quadrant pain, and a striking rise in blood eosinophils, followed by a chronic biliary phase in which adult flukes residing in the bile ducts cause obstruction, cholangitis, and progressive hepatic fibrosis. Triclabendazole is the drug of choice and achieves high cure rates when given promptly.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 2.4 million people infected globally; 180 million at risk (WHO)
keyStats.demographics: Predominates in rural populations in Bolivia, Peru, Egypt, and Iran; affects all ages but children and adults who forage or consume raw watercress are at highest risk
keyStats.avgAge: No strict age peak; children in endemic rural areas and travellers are disproportionately affected
keyStats.globalCases: Over 51 countries report human cases; hyperendemic foci in the Bolivian and Peruvian altiplano and the Nile Delta

definition: Fascioliasis (ICD-10: B66.3) is a foodborne helminthic infection caused by the trematode flukes Fasciola hepatica (the common liver fluke, found globally) and Fasciola gigantica (primarily in sub-Saharan Africa and Asia). Adult flukes inhabit the biliary tree of definitive hosts — usually cattle, sheep, and goats — excreting eggs into freshwater where miracidia hatch and develop through snail intermediate hosts (chiefly Lymnaea species) before emerging as free-swimming cercariae that encyst on aquatic vegetation as metacercariae. Humans are accidental hosts who become infected after ingesting contaminated raw plants such as watercress, alfalfa, mint, or wild sorrel. After ingestion, metacercariae excyst in the duodenum, and young flukes penetrate the intestinal wall to migrate through the peritoneum and liver parenchyma over several weeks before reaching the intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts. The disease falls under infectious disease and gastroenterology and is classified as a neglected tropical disease by the WHO. Immunosuppression and heavy worm burdens worsen outcomes. Ectopic fascioliasis can occur when larvae migrate to the lungs, eye, subcutaneous tissue, or central nervous system.

typesClassification.description: Begins weeks after ingestion while immature flukes migrate through the liver parenchyma; characterised by fever, right-upper-quadrant pain, hepatomegaly, and marked blood eosinophilia (often 30-80%).
Develops once adult flukes settle in the bile ducts; can last for years, causing intermittent biliary colic, cholangitis, cholestasis, and progressive periductal fibrosis.
A period of few or no symptoms between acute and chronic phases as flukes complete migration into the bile ducts.
Rare migration of larvae to atypical sites including lung, skin, eyes, spinal cord, or peritoneum, producing site-specific inflammatory masses.

primarySymptoms: Right-upper-quadrant or epigastric pain — a dull, gnawing ache that may become colicky as adult flukes obstruct bile flow; the hallmark symptom across both phases.
Fever — intermittent, often spiking to 38-40°C during the acute hepatic phase as migrating larvae provoke hepatic necrosis and inflammation.
Marked eosinophilia — blood eosinophil counts typically rise to 30-80% of the leucocyte differential, one of the most diagnostically striking features of acute fascioliasis.
Hepatomegaly — the liver enlarges and becomes tender on palpation, reflecting parenchymal destruction by migrating flukes.
Jaundice — obstructive yellowing of the skin and sclerae develops in the chronic phase when adult flukes or their eggs block the biliary tree.
Nausea, vomiting, and anorexia — gastrointestinal symptoms accompanying the acute inflammatory response and biliary dysfunction.
Urticaria and pruritus — cutaneous manifestations of the intense immune response, particularly during acute migration.
Fatigue and malaise — a non-specific but prominent complaint throughout both phases, especially in heavy infections.
Weight loss — progressive in chronic infection, reflecting reduced appetite, malabsorption, and the metabolic burden of a sustained inflammatory state.
Itching without rash (cholestatic pruritus) — a sign of bile salt deposition in the skin when biliary obstruction is significant.
earlyWarningSigns: A sharp, poorly explained rise in blood eosinophils after travel to or residence in an endemic region.
Right-upper-quadrant discomfort beginning 2-4 weeks after eating watercress or other aquatic plants.
Low-grade intermittent fever with no obvious source in someone who has consumed raw freshwater vegetables.
Mild liver tenderness or enlargement detected incidentally on physical examination.
emergencySigns: High fever with rigors, severe right-upper-quadrant pain, and jaundice suggesting acute ascending cholangitis requiring urgent biliary intervention.
Signs of liver abscess: persistent fever, extreme tenderness, a tender palpable mass, and imaging confirming a fluid collection.
Acute abdominal pain with guarding suggesting perforation or peritoneal involvement by migrating larvae.
Altered consciousness or neurological deficits pointing to ectopic cerebral fascioliasis.

causes.description: The primary route of human infection. Metacercariae encyst on the surface of raw aquatic plants — especially watercress, alfalfa, mint, and wild sorrel. Cooking destroys the larvae, but eating these plants raw in endemic areas is a reliable transmission pathway.

Drinking unfiltered surface water in which metacercariae have detached from vegetation can transmit infection, though this is a less efficient route than plant ingestion because cyst survival in open water is brief.
Rare reports of infection after eating raw or undercooked liver from heavily infected animals (sheep, cattle), where viable metacercariae may transiently survive in biliary tissue.

causes.cause: Contamination of food using infected water for irrigation
causes.description: Irrigation with water containing cercariae from infected snails deposits metacercariae on crops in endemic areas, extending transmission beyond obviously aquatic plants to any produce grown in waterlogged or floodplain soils.

Lymnaea snail populations flourish in wet, marshy freshwater habitats at altitudes of 1,000-4,000 m in the Andes and in irrigated farmland in Egypt and Iran. Dense snail populations amplify the environmental larval burden, raising the per-serving dose of metacercariae on local vegetation.

riskFactors.factor: Living in or travelling to an endemic region
riskFactors.description: Bolivia, Peru, Ecuador, Egypt, and Iran carry the highest human transmission rates; rural altiplano communities in Bolivia record seroprevalence above 70% in some surveys.
Consuming unwashed or raw watercress, alfalfa, or other aquatic greens is the single most modifiable risk. Thorough washing and cooking eliminate metacercariae.

riskFactors.factor: Agricultural or pastoral work with livestock
riskFactors.description: Farmers, shepherds, and livestock workers have repeated contact with contaminated water and vegetation in snail-endemic marshlands, increasing exposure probability.
Children in endemic areas show higher infection rates than adults, likely from unsupervised foraging and play near water sources.

HIV infection, corticosteroid therapy, and haematological malignancies can impair eosinophil-mediated killing of migrating larvae, increasing the likelihood of heavy worm burdens and ectopic migration.

riskFactors.factor: Absence of systematic livestock treatment programmes
riskFactors.description: In countries lacking routine veterinary anthelmintic programmes, high livestock fluke burdens maintain a large environmental egg reservoir, sustaining snail infection rates and human exposure.

affectedDemographics: Rural populations in Bolivia, Peru, and Ecuador — some of the highest per-capita incidences globally
Agricultural communities in Egypt and Iran, where irrigation farming intersects with Lymnaea habitats
Children aged 5-15 in hyperendemic communities, who often have the heaviest worm burdens
Travellers and expatriates returning from endemic regions who ate local produce raw
People consuming organically grown watercress or watercress from natural streams in Europe and the Americas
diagnosisOverview: Fascioliasis is diagnosed by combining clinical features, epidemiological exposure, and laboratory findings. During the acute phase, serology is the cornerstone because eggs are not yet present in stool; ELISA testing for Fasciola-specific antibodies achieves sensitivity above 95% in reference laboratories during active migration. Blood eosinophilia exceeding 30% of the differential, together with raised liver enzymes (ALT, AST, ALP) and hepatomegaly, strongly supports the diagnosis in a person with relevant food or travel history. Abdominal ultrasound and CT in the acute phase may show characteristic hypoechoic hepatic lesions and subcapsular tunnelling along the fluke migration path. In the chronic phase, eggs can be detected in stool or duodenal aspirate by standard microscopy (400 µm operculated eggs with a shoulder at the aboral end); however, stool microscopy has limited sensitivity in light infections. Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) can visualise adult flukes in the bile ducts and simultaneously extract them. Serology remains positive for months after cure, so it cannot distinguish active from past infection without serial titres. Cross-reactivity with other trematodes (Fasciola gigantica, Clonorchis) occurs and may require species-specific confirmatory testing in co-endemic areas.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects IgG antibodies against Fasciola excretory-secretory antigens; the most sensitive test during the acute phase before egg shedding begins.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results from a reference laboratory typically take several days. Sensitivity exceeds 95% in the acute phase; cross-reactivity with other flukes is a recognised limitation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the characteristic large operculated Fasciola eggs in the faeces of patients in the chronic biliary phase.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You collect a stool sample at home; concentrated wet-preparation slides are examined under a microscope in the laboratory. At least three samples collected on separate days are recommended to improve sensitivity.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies eosinophilia, which is a highly suggestive finding — often 30-80% of white cells — during acute infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw; results are available within a few hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver function tests (ALT, AST, ALP, bilirubin)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Reflects hepatic inflammation during the acute phase and biliary obstruction or cholestasis in the chronic phase.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Part of a standard blood panel, available within hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies hepatic lesions, subcapsular haematoma-like tracks, bile duct dilation, or flukes visible in the bile ducts on CT as hypodense filling defects.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Ultrasound is non-invasive and takes 20-30 minutes; abdominal CT requires lying still in a scanner and may use iodinated contrast.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualises and sometimes directly extracts adult flukes from the biliary tree in chronic cases with confirmed bile duct obstruction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A sedated endoscopic procedure lasting 30-90 minutes; it carries a small risk of post-procedure pancreatitis and is reserved for cases where both diagnosis and biliary clearance are needed.

treatmentOverview: Triclabendazole is the WHO-recommended first-line treatment for fascioliasis and is effective against both juvenile and adult flukes. The standard regimen is a single dose of 10 mg/kg orally with a meal, though some experts give 10 mg/kg twice in one day for heavy infections, and 20 mg/kg in two divided doses over two days is used for treatment failures. Triclabendazole achieves parasitological cure in approximately 80-95% of patients, with eosinophil counts and antibody titres declining over weeks to months. Nitazoxanide is a second-line option when triclabendazole is unavailable or fails, with reported cure rates around 60-75%; bithionol was formerly used but is rarely available and poorly tolerated. In the chronic biliary phase, management additionally focuses on relieving biliary obstruction. ERCP with sphincterotomy or bile duct extraction of adult flukes may be required before or alongside anthelmintic therapy when cholangitis or jaundice is significant. Secondary bacterial cholangitis is treated with appropriate antibiotics. Ectopic disease may require surgical excision of inflammatory masses, particularly in the subcutaneous or ophthalmic sites. Supportive measures — rest, analgesia, hydration, and nutritional support — help during the acute febrile phase. Follow-up stool examination and serological monitoring are performed at 3 and 6 months to confirm cure, since a single negative stool does not exclude residual infection.

medicalTreatments.name: Triclabendazole
medicalTreatments.description: The WHO essential medicine of choice; a benzimidazole-related compound that disrupts fluke tubulin and tegument. Given as 10 mg/kg orally with food in a single dose, or split over two doses for severe or drug-resistant cases. It is active against both immature migrating and adult biliary flukes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Parasitological cure in approximately 80-95% of patients after one course; a second course achieves cure in the majority of initial non-responders.

medicalTreatments.name: Nitazoxanide
medicalTreatments.description: A thiazolide antiparasitic used as second-line therapy when triclabendazole is contraindicated or not available. Doses of 500 mg twice daily for 7 days in adults have been used in treatment protocols.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates reported at approximately 60-75% in case series; notably inferior to triclabendazole but a useful alternative.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotics for secondary cholangitis
medicalTreatments.description: When bacterial superinfection of the bile ducts accompanies chronic fascioliasis, broad-spectrum antibiotics targeting gram-negative organisms (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam, ciprofloxacin) are added to anthelmintic therapy to prevent sepsis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for treating bacterial component; biliary decompression combined with antibiotics resolves cholangitis in the large majority of cases.

medicalTreatments.name: ERCP with sphincterotomy or fluke extraction
medicalTreatments.description: An endoscopic procedure that relieves biliary obstruction caused by adult flukes or eggs. Can be combined with extraction of flukes directly from the common bile duct. Most useful in chronic disease with obstructive jaundice or cholangitis refractory to drug treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Technical success in clearing the biliary tree exceeds 90% in experienced centres; reduces complications of obstruction and accelerates clinical recovery in 1-2 days.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary precautions
alternativeTreatments.description: Avoiding raw aquatic plants from endemic areas and cooking freshwater vegetables thoroughly eliminates the transmission route and is the foundation of prevention in endemic communities.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nutritional support during recovery
alternativeTreatments.description: High-protein, energy-adequate diets support hepatic regeneration after parenchymal damage from migrating larvae; micronutrient supplementation including iron and vitamin B12 may be indicated if malabsorption or anaemia is documented.

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease specialist or tropical medicine physician should be involved whenever fascioliasis is confirmed or strongly suspected. Triclabendazole is not widely stocked in standard pharmacies and requires specialist access in many countries. Chronic biliary disease with obstruction or cholangitis additionally requires a gastroenterologist or hepatologist with ERCP capability. Ectopic fascioliasis involving unusual sites such as the eye, brain, or spinal cord warrants multidisciplinary input including ophthalmology or neurosurgery.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an infectious disease specialist or tropical medicine physician with experience managing foodborne helminths and access to triclabendazole through a tropical disease formulary or WHO essential medicines programme. For chronic biliary complications, choose a gastroenterology or hepatology unit where ERCP is available. If you acquired infection while travelling, a travel medicine clinic with a dedicated helminthology interest can co-ordinate both diagnosis and drug procurement.
preventionStrategies: Cook all freshwater aquatic vegetables — particularly watercress — thoroughly before eating; temperatures above 60°C for at least one minute destroy metacercariae.
Avoid drinking untreated surface water in endemic areas, especially from ponds, streams, or marshes where Lymnaea snails breed.
Wash hands and produce carefully after handling vegetables grown or irrigated in potentially contaminated water.
In endemic livestock-farming communities, implement regular anthelmintic treatment of cattle and sheep to reduce the environmental fluke egg burden.
Support public health programmes targeting Lymnaea snail habitats through water management, drainage improvement, and molluscicide application where ecologically appropriate.
Screen populations in high-incidence communities using serology or stool surveys to identify and treat infected individuals before chronic complications develop.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid raw watercress or aquatic greens purchased from open-air markets in Bolivia, Peru, Egypt, or Iran.
When foraging, collect plants only from certified, flowing, clean-water cultivation and always cook them.
Maintain adequate nutrition and avoid alcohol to support hepatic recovery.
If you have livestock, consult a vet about regular fluke treatment programmes and proper disposal of manure away from water sources.
Travel to endemic regions with awareness of food-safety rules for water and produce.

recommended: Thoroughly cooked vegetables, including any formerly raw watercress or aquatic greens
High-protein foods to support hepatic repair during the recovery phase
Iron-rich foods or supplements if anaemia develops from chronic blood loss or reduced absorption
Adequate fluid intake to support biliary flow and recovery
avoid: Raw or inadequately washed aquatic plants (watercress, alfalfa, mint, wild sorrel) from endemic areas or uncertain water sources
Untreated surface or stream water in endemic regions
Excess alcohol, which compounds hepatotoxic stress during active liver inflammation
Raw or undercooked sheep or cattle liver from endemic areas

exerciseGuidelines: During the acute febrile phase with hepatomegaly and liver tenderness, strenuous exercise should be avoided because of the risk of exacerbating pain and hepatic strain. Light ambulation is encouraged once fever settles. After confirmed parasitological cure and return of liver function tests to normal — typically 2-4 months post-treatment — normal physical activity can be resumed progressively. Fatigue is common during treatment and for several weeks afterwards; pace activity accordingly.
dailyManagement: Complete the full prescribed triclabendazole course even if symptoms begin to improve after the first dose.
Return for follow-up stool examination and serology at 3 and 6 months post-treatment to confirm cure.
Report new or worsening right-upper-quadrant pain, jaundice, or fever promptly — these may signal a second treatment course is needed or that biliary complications have developed.
Monitor for signs of iron-deficiency anaemia and take prescribed supplements consistently.
Notify household members who shared the implicated meal to be assessed for possible co-infection.
prognosis: With prompt diagnosis and triclabendazole treatment, the prognosis of fascioliasis is excellent. Acute infection resolves in the great majority of patients within weeks of effective therapy, eosinophilia normalises over 3-6 months, and liver function tests return to baseline. Untreated or late-diagnosed chronic fascioliasis carries a worse outlook: prolonged biliary obstruction leads to periductal fibrosis, secondary biliary cirrhosis, and portal hypertension in a minority of patients, especially those with heavy worm burdens or repeated reinfection. Secondary bacterial cholangitis and, rarely, biliary carcinoma have been reported after decades of chronic fluke infestation. Mortality in treated disease is negligible; in complicated untreated chronic disease with biliary cirrhosis, outcomes depend on the degree of hepatic damage. Reinfection can occur after cure if exposure continues, so re-treatment and sustained prevention measures are essential in endemic communities.
recoveryTimeline: Fever and hepatic tenderness typically begin to improve within 3-7 days of triclabendazole therapy. Blood eosinophil counts decline over 4-8 weeks but may remain mildly elevated for several months. Imaging abnormalities in the liver gradually resolve over 3-6 months. Full parasitological cure, confirmed by negative stool microscopy and declining antibody titres, is established at the 6-month follow-up visit. Biliary complications such as ductal fibrosis may take 6-12 months to stabilise and may not fully reverse if structural damage was extensive.
complications: Secondary bacterial cholangitis — bacterial superinfection of the obstructed bile ducts, requiring antibiotics and biliary drainage on an urgent basis.
Biliary cirrhosis and portal hypertension — results from years of untreated chronic obstruction and periductal fibrosis; manifests as ascites, variceal bleeding, and liver failure.
Ectopic fascioliasis — migration of larvae to subcutaneous tissue, lungs, eye, or central nervous system, causing abscesses or inflammatory pseudotumours at non-hepatic sites.
Anaemia and malnutrition — from chronic blood loss in the bile ducts and impaired intestinal absorption in heavy long-standing infections.
Pancreatitis — flukes migrating into or near the pancreatic duct trigger acute pancreatitis in rare cases.
Cholangiocarcinoma (rare) — long-standing biliary epithelial inflammation from chronic infection has been associated with bile duct cancer, as with other biliary trematodes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Clonorchiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Clonorchis sinensis also infects the bile ducts but is transmitted by eating raw freshwater fish, not plants, and is limited to East Asia; eggs are smaller and narrower than Fasciola eggs.

confusedWithConditions.name: Opisthorchiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Opisthorchis flukes similarly cause biliary disease but are acquired from raw fish in Southeast Asia and Central Europe; they produce smaller eggs and less dramatic eosinophilia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Echinococcus granulosus (hepatic)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hepatic echinococcosis produces cystic liver lesions on imaging that can mimic fascioliasis abscesses, but serology and cyst characteristics on CT differentiate them; eosinophilia is usually milder.

confusedWithConditions.name: Amebiasis (hepatic)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Amebic liver abscess causes right-upper-quadrant pain and fever but eosinophilia is absent or mild, serology is directed at Entamoeba histolytica, and the abscess is a fluid-filled cavity rather than a track lesion.

relatedConditions.name: Clonorchiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another foodborne biliary fluke infection sharing bile duct pathology and similar anthelmintic treatment principles.

relatedConditions.name: Opisthorchiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A closely related foodborne trematode infection of the bile ducts; distinguishing features include geographic distribution and transmission route.

relatedConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A trematode infection causing chronic hepatic fibrosis and portal hypertension through a different mechanism; often overlaps geographically with fascioliasis.

relatedConditions.name: Echinococcosis
relatedConditions.relevance: A cestode infection producing hepatic cysts that may radiologically resemble early fascioliasis lesions in the liver.

faqs.question: What is fascioliasis?
faqs.answer: Fascioliasis is a parasitic infection caused by liver flukes (Fasciola hepatica or F. gigantica). People become infected by eating raw aquatic plants like watercress that carry the parasite's larvae. The flukes migrate through the liver into the bile ducts, causing pain, fever, and liver inflammation.

faqs.question: How do people get fascioliasis?
faqs.answer: The infection is acquired almost entirely by eating raw or inadequately cooked aquatic plants — especially watercress — contaminated with infective Fasciola larvae (metacercariae). Drinking unfiltered water from endemic streams is a less common route. The parasite is not spread directly from person to person.

faqs.question: Where in the world is fascioliasis most common?
faqs.answer: The highest human infection rates are in the Bolivian and Peruvian altiplano, where community seroprevalence can exceed 70%. Other high-burden countries include Peru, Egypt, Iran, and parts of Portugal and France. Over 51 countries report human cases worldwide.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of a liver fluke infection?
faqs.answer: Early symptoms typically appear 2-4 weeks after eating contaminated plants and include fever, right-sided upper abdominal pain, and fatigue. A blood test at this stage usually shows a dramatically elevated eosinophil count — often 30-80% of white blood cells — which is one of the most reliable early clues.

faqs.question: Is fascioliasis dangerous?
faqs.answer: Acute fascioliasis can cause significant liver damage and biliary disease, but it is curable with timely treatment. The main danger is delayed diagnosis, which allows adult flukes to cause chronic bile duct obstruction, fibrosis, and in rare prolonged cases, secondary biliary cirrhosis or bile duct cancer. With prompt triclabendazole therapy, outcomes are excellent.

faqs.question: How is fascioliasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: In the acute phase, a blood test for Fasciola-specific antibodies (ELISA serology) is the most sensitive test because eggs are not yet present in stool. Raised eosinophils and liver enzymes support the diagnosis. In the chronic phase, stool microscopy can detect the large characteristic eggs. Ultrasound or CT shows liver lesions or bile duct dilation.

faqs.question: What drug treats fascioliasis?
faqs.answer: Triclabendazole is the WHO-recommended treatment: a single oral dose of 10 mg/kg taken with a meal. It works against both young migrating and adult biliary flukes and achieves cure in roughly 80-95% of patients. A second course is given if follow-up stool examination remains positive at 3 months.

faqs.question: Can fascioliasis be cured completely?
faqs.answer: Yes. Most patients are completely cured with one or two courses of triclabendazole, confirmed by negative stool microscopy and declining antibody titres at 3-6 months. However, structural damage to the bile ducts from years of untreated chronic infection may not fully reverse, and reinfection can occur if exposure continues.

faqs.question: Can fascioliasis spread from person to person?
faqs.answer: No. Fascioliasis is not contagious between people. The infection requires an intermediate snail host and contaminated aquatic plants to complete its transmission cycle. You cannot catch it from another infected person through normal contact.

faqs.question: What does a high eosinophil count mean in fascioliasis?
faqs.answer: Blood eosinophils rise dramatically — often to 30-80% of white cells — because the immune system responds to the migrating fluke larvae with a strong allergic-type reaction. This eosinophilia is one of the most diagnostically helpful signs of acute fascioliasis and typically normalises over 4-8 weeks after successful treatment.

faqs.question: How long does fascioliasis last without treatment?
faqs.answer: Untreated, the acute phase may subside as flukes complete migration into the bile ducts, but the infection persists indefinitely. Adult flukes can survive in the biliary tree for years, causing progressive obstruction and fibrosis. Without treatment, chronic biliary complications develop in a significant proportion of infected individuals.

faqs.question: Is watercress safe to eat?
faqs.answer: Commercially farmed watercress grown in controlled, clean-water systems is generally safe. The risk arises from wild-gathered or stream-grown watercress in areas where infected sheep or cattle graze nearby, especially in endemic countries. Cooking watercress thoroughly eliminates any risk.

faqs.question: Can children get fascioliasis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children in endemic rural communities often have higher infection rates and heavier worm burdens than adults, partly because of unsupervised foraging near water sources. Children may also present more atypically, with abdominal pain and growth faltering rather than the classic adult presentation of fever and jaundice.

faqs.question: What blood tests show fascioliasis?
faqs.answer: The most useful blood tests are: (1) Fasciola ELISA serology (antibody test) — sensitive above 95% in the acute phase; (2) full blood count showing elevated eosinophils; (3) liver function tests showing raised ALT, AST, and alkaline phosphatase. None is pathognomonic alone, but together with food exposure history they are highly suggestive.

faqs.question: Can fascioliasis affect the lungs?
faqs.answer: Rarely, larvae migrate ectopically to the lungs rather than the liver, causing pulmonary fascioliasis with cough, breathlessness, and pulmonary infiltrates on chest X-ray. This represents less than 1% of cases but should be considered in patients with unexplained lung infiltrates and high eosinophilia alongside a relevant travel and food history.

faqs.question: How soon after eating contaminated food do symptoms appear?
faqs.answer: Symptoms of the acute phase typically begin 2-4 weeks after ingestion, when immature flukes start their migration through the liver parenchyma. Some heavily infected people develop symptoms within 1-2 weeks. The chronic biliary phase begins 3-4 months after infection and may persist for years.

faqs.question: Does fascioliasis cause jaundice?
faqs.answer: Jaundice develops primarily in the chronic phase when adult flukes residing in the bile ducts cause obstructive cholestasis. It is less common in the acute phase but can occur if inflammation causes substantial biliary compression. New-onset jaundice in a patient being treated for fascioliasis warrants urgent assessment for cholangitis.

faqs.question: Can fascioliasis be prevented by a vaccine?
faqs.answer: No licensed human vaccine exists for fascioliasis. Prevention relies entirely on avoiding contaminated raw aquatic plants and water, implementing food hygiene measures, and controlling the disease in livestock. Veterinary vaccines against Fasciola hepatica are under development for use in cattle and sheep to reduce the environmental reservoir.

faqs.question: Is triclabendazole available everywhere?
faqs.answer: Triclabendazole is not routinely stocked in standard pharmacies in most non-endemic countries. In the United States and Europe, it requires a specialist prescription through tropical medicine services or expanded-access programmes. In endemic countries including Bolivia, Peru, and Egypt, it is more widely available through national health programmes.

faqs.question: What happens to the liver after fascioliasis treatment?
faqs.answer: Liver enzyme abnormalities and imaging lesions typically resolve within 3-6 months of successful treatment, and the liver recovers well in most patients. Severe inflammation tracks visible on CT usually disappear within 6 months. If chronic biliary fibrosis was already established before treatment, some structural changes may persist but further progression is halted.

metaDescription: Fascioliasis is a liver fluke infection from raw watercress causing fever, liver pain, and eosinophilia. Learn diagnosis and triclabendazole treatment.
